findings.description: Bare `except: pass` in transform_batch silently discards transformation errors with no logging, no error count increment, and no signal to the caller. Failed records vanish without trace.
findings.evidence: except Exception:\n            pass — no logging, no _error_count increment, no re-raise. A bad rate conversion, a key error, or a type error will be swallowed; the pipeline reports success.

findings.description: normalize_currency returns the unconverted record when a currency rate is missing, allowing non-USD amounts to flow through as if they were USD. The log warning is the only signal; the record is not filtered, flagged, or counted as an error.
findings.evidence:
rate = rates.get(currency)
if rate is None:
    logger.warning(...)
    return record  # returns unconverted record silently — comment is the author acknowledging the problem

findings.description: Race condition: multiple threads call `all_results.extend(transformed)` and `_processed_count += 1` without any lock. list.extend is not thread-safe under all CPython scenarios and _processed_count (read-modify-write) is not atomic.
findings.evidence: `global _processed_count` incremented with `+= 1` inside threads; `all_results.extend(transformed)` on a shared list inside threads — no threading.Lock anywhere in the file.

findings.description: _processed_count is a module-level global reset at the start of run_pipeline. If run_pipeline is called concurrently (multiple pipelines), the counters from different runs interfere with each other.
findings.evidence: Module-level `_processed_count = 0` / `_error_count = 0` reset inside `run_pipeline` — not instance state, not protected, reused globally.

findings.description: validate_transaction is defined and exported but never called anywhere in the pipeline. Dead validation code means schema violations silently reach the database write stage.
findings.evidence: validate_transaction exists but transform_batch calls only normalize_currency; no call site in run_pipeline.

findings.description: When num_workers does not evenly divide len(raw_records), the last batch is handled correctly, but if num_workers > len(raw_records) the computed batch_size is 0 (integer division), causing all workers except the last to receive empty batches and the last worker to receive all records — effectively single-threaded with the overhead of N-1 extra threads.
findings.evidence: batch_size = len(raw_records) // num_workers — integer floor division; if len < num_workers, batch_size = 0.
